title: Yoga and heart rate variability: A comprehensive review of the literature
headline: Yoga and heart rate variability: A comprehensive review of the literature
summary: A randomized controlled trial of yoga examined brain & mechanisms. Regular yoga practitioners were also found to have increased vagal tone at rest compared to non-yoga practitioners. It is premature to draw any firm conclusions about yoga and HRV as most studies were of poor quality, with small sample sizes and insufficient reporting of study design and statistical methods.
keyFindings: The reviewed studies suggest that yoga can affect cardiac autonomic regulation with increased HRV and vagal dominance during yoga practices.
Regular yoga practitioners were also found to have increased vagal tone at rest compared to non-yoga practitioners.
It is premature to draw any firm conclusions about yoga and HRV as most studies were of poor quality, with small sample sizes and insufficient reporting of study design and statistical methods.
limitations: It is premature to draw any firm conclusions about yoga and HRV as most studies were of poor quality, with small sample sizes and insufficient reporting of study design and statistical methods.
Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.
The practice studied (yoga) is not the Miracle of Mind practice; findings do not transfer directly to MoM.
simplifiedTitle: Yoga and heart rate variability: A comprehensive review of the literature
aiSnippet: RCT, International journal of yoga (2016). The reviewed studies suggest that yoga can affect cardiac autonomic regulation with increased HRV and vagal dominance during yoga practices. Regular yoga practitioners were also found to have increased vagal tone at rest compared to non-yoga practitioners. It is premature to draw any firm conclusions about yoga and HRV as most studies were of poor quality, with small sample sizes and insufficient reporting of study design and statistical methods.
mechanismGap: The practice in this study does not share a specific breath-regulation or directed-attention mechanism with the Miracle of Mind practice, so no practice bridge is drawn here.
